---
date: 2025-10-08
title: "Creating a point cloud with a TF-Luna LiDAR and a pan-tilt assembly"
keywords: [
    "Raspberry Pi",
    "TF-Luna",
    "LiDAR",
    "Pan-Tilt",
    "Rust",
    "Rerun",
]
---

::: {.callout-note}
The full code for this project/experiment can be found in the following repository:
[rpi-tfluna-lidar](https://github.com/AnesBenmerzoug/rpi-tfluna-lidar)
:::

In [the previous TF-Luna post](../rpi-tfluna-lidar/), we saw what the TF-Luna LiDAR distance sensor is
and how to use it with a Raspberry Pi.

In the meantime, I developed and published the [embedded-tfluna](https://crates.io/crates/embedded-tfluna) crate
specifically for the sensor. It is platform agnostic and works in no-std environments.

In this post, we will create a point cloud by mounting the sensor on a pan-tilt assembly with two servo motors
and taking a measurement at each position of the assembly.

A point cloud is a discrete set of data points in space. The points may represent a 3D shape or object.
Each point position has its set of Cartesian coordinates (X, Y, Z).
Points may contain data other than position such as RGB colors, normals, timestamps and others.
Point clouds are generally produced by 3D scanners or by photogrammetry software, which measure many points on the external surfaces of objects around them. 

Point clouds are used for many purposes, including to create 3D computer-aided design (CAD) or geographic information systems (GIS) models for manufactured parts, for metrology and quality inspection, and for a multitude of visualizing, animating, rendering, and mass customization applications. 

![Picture of the TF-Luna mounted on the pan-tilt assembly and connected to the Raspberry Pi](raspberry_pi_with_tfluna_and_pan_tilt.jpg){width=80%}


## Pan-Tilt Mechanism

For the pan-tilt mechanism, I first started by ordering a cheap pan-tilt mechanism from aliexpress.

The two micro servos that came with it were unfortunately either broken or defect and wouldn't hold their position.
They instead kept rotating continuously.

After that, I gave up on it and decided to order a better mechanism instead which I did.

That one unfortunately also came with a continuous rotation servo which I couldn't use.

I ended up using two other servos that I had laying ordered and was planning to use in another project.

## Point Cloud


## Conclusion

We could take this to the limit and see how quickly we can create the point cloud.
